hypothesis: Setting up CPE Procurement Analytics for Helios Telecom
what_we_did: Created project scaffold with dbt+DuckDB+Streamlit
what_happened: Project structure created with knowledge wiki infrastructure
conclusion: Framework ready for SAP sample data generation and Data Vault modeling

hypothesis: Generate realistic SAP MM sample data for HT CPE procurement
what_we_did: Created Python script generating 32 tables with referential integrity
what_happened: Data covers 2024-01 to 2026-03 with 45K CPE devices and 2200 POs and 32K material movements
conclusion: Sample data loaded into raw_sap schema in DuckDB. All 4 RI checks pass. Avg lead time 44.8d. Ready for staging layer.

hypothesis: Document custom ABAP code for HT SAP system
what_we_did: Created 16 ABAP program entries and 11 Z-table entries covering CPE lifecycle automation and procurement rules
what_happened: Wiki now shows complete custom code catalog with table dependency graph and risk levels
conclusion: ABAP documentation layer complete — covers serial validation equipment lifecycle provisioning bridge vendor scoring warranty tracking and financial depreciation. In real engagement this would be auto-populated by Claude scanning exported ABAP source.

hypothesis: Complete the serial number chain (SERI SER01 SER03 OBJK) for full CPE traceability
what_we_did: Generated 4 serial number tables linking individual devices to GR documents and POs
what_happened: Serial chain complete: EQUI ↔ OBJK ↔ SERI ↔ MKPF/MSEG ↔ EKKO/EKPO. Full device-to-PO traceability.
conclusion: Data Vault link_equipment_po can now be built from OBJK+SER01. 5 tables intentionally empty (MCHB MVKE MSKA LQUA LTAP) — documented in known_decisions.

hypothesis: Document why 5 SAP tables are intentionally empty in the MVP
what_we_did: Created empty table schemas for MCHB MVKE MSKA LQUA LTAP with correct column structure
what_happened: MCHB: CPE is serial not batch managed. MVKE: CPE procured not sold via SD. MSKA: no sales order stock. LQUA/LTAP: WM module not active at HT.
conclusion: Tables exist for schema completeness and data dictionary validity. Will be populated if scope expands to include WM or SD modules.

hypothesis: Create staging layer following strict DWH best practice: 1:1 with source tables
what_we_did: Built 36 staging views — one per populated raw SAP table. Original column names preserved. Only adds hash keys hashdiff record_source load_date and type casting. No joins no renaming no business logic.
what_happened: All models pass. 24 data tests pass (not_null + unique on hash keys and business keys). Row counts match 1:1 with source. Hash keys consistent across models referencing same business keys.
conclusion: Staging follows purist Data Vault approach: mechanical transformation only. Business naming and logic deferred to vault layer. Dropped hk_po_vendor from stg_sap__ekpo — vault-time resolution via EKKO join.

hypothesis: Build Data Vault 2.0 layer: 8 hubs 9 links 16 satellites
what_we_did: Implemented full DV2.0 on DuckDB with dbt incremental models. Business naming in satellites. Hash key joins throughout. All vault objects read from staging only.
what_happened: All 33 models pass. 61 data tests pass. All link-to-hub references valid (0 orphans across 5 checks). 8777 equipment devices traceable end-to-end: device -> GR -> PO -> vendor via vault links.
conclusion: Data Vault complete. 33 incremental models (spec said 32 but listed 16 satellites so actual total is 33). Hubs insert-only. Satellites use hashdiff for SCD2 change detection. Fixed link_equipment_gr to hash MBLNR+MJAHR via SERI->MKPF join so hk_material_document matches hub definition. Ready for marts layer.

hypothesis: Build Kimball star schema (marts) and OBT flattened views on top of Data Vault
what_we_did: Created 7 dims 5 facts 5 OBT views (17 models). Business metrics from glossary (BG001 lead time BG002 OTD BG008 cycle time) computed in fact_purchase_orders. Vault-to-mart join pattern: hub + latest satellite + links.
what_happened: All 17 models pass. 22 tests pass. BG001=44.8d BG002=55.5% BG008=4.0d. Equipment: 24857 deployed 18000 in_stock 1495 returned 648 defective. Huawei EUR28.2M top spend.
conclusion: Full analytical stack complete: raw -> staging -> vault -> marts -> OBT. Fixed three spec bugs: hk_po_item vs hk_po_material mismatch gr_totals hk_material_document join fact_invoices hk_vendor not in sat.

hypothesis: Create 5-page Streamlit dashboard reading from OBT views
what_we_did: Built dashboard.py + db.py + 5 pages under app/pages: 01 procurement_overview 02 vendor_scorecard 03 cpe_lifecycle 04 inventory_health 05 goods_movements. Dark theme. Sidebar filters. KPI cards + Plotly charts + detail tables.
what_happened: All 5 pages syntax-validated. All 5 OBT SELECT queries resolve. Streamlit server starts on 8501 returns 200 OK and health=ok. Each page reads from one OBT view only - no complex joins in Python.
conclusion: Dashboard MVP complete. Moved pandas import to file top on procurement_overview page (spec had it after first pd.notna call which would have crashed). Installed streamlit and plotly in venv.

hypothesis: Create knowledge layer: 6 computed views for live procurement health vendor performance CPE lifecycle inventory alerts data quality and pipeline summary
what_we_did: Built 6 knowledge models + updated session_context.sql + full export_context.py + ran data profiling. Knowledge models compute health status GREEN/YELLOW/RED for all key metrics.
what_happened: All 6 models pass. 8 DQ checks: 7 PASS 1 WARN (vendor_concentration). Procurement health: OTD 54.5% RED lead_time 44.8d YELLOW cycle 4.0d GREEN concentration Huawei 85.1% RED. 5 context files generated. profile_data 5/10 OK 5 errors (stale config refs to fact_equipment_movements fact_inventory_snapshots raw_ekko raw_mkpf days_of_stock column).
conclusion: Intelligence layer complete. Session startup now surfaces live system state with health assessments. Context export ready for chat sessions.

hypothesis: Move DQ checks from knowledge model to proper dbt tests
what_we_did: knowledge_data_quality was a knowledge model but DQ is not business knowledge — it is data validation. Deleted the model and migrated 8 checks to dbt/tests/ as custom singular tests. Updated session_context.sql and export_context.py to reference dbt test instead.
what_happened: Knowledge layer now 5 models (was 6). 8 singular tests run via dbt test: 7 PASS 1 FAIL. assert_vendor_concentration_below_60pct correctly FAILS with 9 Huawei quarters violating PR004 — stronger enforcement than the old WARN-only view (already documented in signal_relationships row 1 as a known policy gap the data surfaces).
conclusion: DQ = dbt tests (pass/fail at build time). Knowledge = business state (queryable views). Never mix these concerns.

hypothesis: Build data governance UI in Streamlit
what_we_did: Created 2 new pages: 06_business_glossary (term detail with source/target profiling live lineage ABAP cross-reference and approve/deny workflow) and 07_data_lineage (architecture overview DV design ABAP catalog SAP dictionary).
what_happened: All 7 pages syntax-validated. All seed and knowledge queries resolve. Streamlit server boots healthy on 8501. Business owner can browse terms view S2T mapping (plain language + SQL) live source/target profiles review lineage and approve/deny with comments. Data analyst can browse ABAP code DV design and full SAP data dictionary.
conclusion: Fixed page 7 layer->pipeline matching (spec had substring bug that missed vault/knowledge). Added denied status color. denied status is new enum — build_knowledge_wiki.py buckets only approved/draft so denied terms invisible in wiki index (cosmetic backlog).

hypothesis: Integrate Claude API into Streamlit for AI-assisted S2T mapping
what_we_did: Added app/claude_api.py that POSTs business term + SAP dictionary + ABAP catalog + existing glossary + existing S2T to api.anthropic.com/v1/messages. Response parsed as structured JSON (sources/joins/filters/transformation_plain/sql/target/warnings/confidence). Page 06 unmapped-term branch renders Ask-Claude button; suggestion appears as editable tables + text areas; Accept writes rows to s2t_mapping.csv.
what_happened: Syntax/import checks pass. Graceful degradation confirmed: no API key returns {error: 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not set'} without network call. Streamlit boots healthy on 8501. .env confirmed in .gitignore at line 5. Installed requests into venv.
conclusion: AI-powered governance differentiator complete. Claude replaces manual SAP consulting work of identifying source tables and writing transformation logic. Combined with auto-profiling and approval workflow the Collibra replacement is end-to-end.

hypothesis: Align all dashboard metrics with business glossary
what_we_did: Added 15 new business terms (BG011-BG025) and 16 new S2T mapping rows. Created components/metric_card.py with metric_with_info() helper. Updated 5 analytical pages to replace st.metric with metric_with_info — every KPI now has either a glossary term_id (full tooltip with definition + sources + transformation) or an inline help_text. Also changed sidebar Data loaded timestamp to honest 'Sample data: 2024-01 to 2026-03'.
what_happened: Business Glossary now has 25 terms (was 10). S2T mapping has 33 rows (was 17). Every dashboard metric has either a business term ID linked or an inline help string. Sidebar branding no longer implies live data refresh.
conclusion: Self-documenting data product complete. Any business user can hover any KPI and see definition + source tables + transformation in one tooltip. Honest sample data caveat in sidebar.

hypothesis: Business users and data analysts need different views of the same business term
what_we_did: Split Term Detail into two tabs: business-friendly Term Detail (plain language lineage, summary profiling) and technical S2T Specification (6-node flow diagram, source tables, SAP dict, SQL, ABAP, full profiling, Ask-Claude fallback). Shared term selector via st.session_state keeps the selected term in sync across tabs.
what_happened: Business users get an uncluttered view with no SQL/table/layer jargon; analysts retain every technical artifact in one place. Visual QA screenshots saved for BG001 (mapped) and BG007 TCO (unmapped) in both views.
conclusion: Two-audience split is the default pattern for any governance UI in this project. Never collapse back into a single tab.

hypothesis: Resolve DV 2.0 orphan-grain finding V6c — sat_po_schedule and sat_po_account had no parent link exposing hk_po_item
what_we_did: Created dbt/models/vault/link_po_item.sql as a unit-of-work link with PK hk_po_item and parent hk_purchase_order sourced from stg_sap__ekpo (EBELN+EBELP grain). Added schema.yml entry with not_null+unique on hk_po_item and not_null on hk_purchase_order. Ran dbt run + dbt test — all 3 tests PASS. Verified all 3 orphan checks return 0.
what_happened: link_po_item has 2200 rows matching both satellites. Both sat_po_schedule and sat_po_account now reach a proper parent via hub → link_po_item → satellite. Vault is fully self-contained — no more staging piercing required for downstream joins.
conclusion: Every PO-item grain satellite must hang off link_po_item. Never create a satellite parented on a hash key that does not exist as a PK in a hub or link.

hypothesis: Never automate business_glossary.status changes — technical deployment and business approval are separate concerns
what_we_did: Deploy-models button initially auto-set status=approved when user only asked for S2T + dbt model creation. Automated approval bypasses the human governance review that the status field represents.
what_happened: Follow-up fix required: removed glossary status change from deploy button. Approval remains a manual data owner action via the glossary edit form.
conclusion: Technical deployment (S2T rows + dbt models + pipeline) must never touch business_glossary.status. That field is a human governance gate. An approved flag set by code is worthless governance theatre.

hypothesis: end_of_task.py now seeds scanner outputs (dbt_column_lineage dbt_model_catalog dbt_model_relationships) into DuckDB before Parquet export
what_we_did: Scanner CSVs were written to disk but not loaded into DuckDB. Parquet export read stale DuckDB tables so Streamlit showed no column lineage for newly deployed models.
what_happened: Added dbt seed --select step between scanner writes and Parquet export in end_of_task.py. All callers of end_of_task.py now get fresh lineage data without manual intervention.
conclusion: end_of_task.py is self-contained: scan -> seed scanner outputs -> export Parquet. No manual dbt seed needed after running it.

hypothesis: Added profiling cache + LLM SQL self-healing retry to Data Analysis page
what_we_did: Guided Analysis re-profiled tables from scratch for every term. SQL errors from hallucinated columns (e.g. BUDAT in MSEG) left users stuck. Added: (1) profiling_cache scope in data_qa_log.csv keyed by tables_used so profile queries are shared across terms (2) llm_retry_on_error() in claude_api.py that captures SQL errors and asks Claude to fix using actual schema from information_schema (3) silent retry UX with subtle caption on success.
what_happened: Profiling cache avoids redundant LLM calls. SQL retry heals column hallucinations silently. data_qa_log.csv extended with scope/retry_count/original_sql columns.
conclusion: Cross-term profiling cache lives in data_qa_log with scope=profiling. SQL retry max 3 attempts with actual schema context. Silent UX — no intermediate retry messages.

hypothesis: Deploy models button runs real dbt pipeline with atomic rollback
what_we_did: Replaced fake file-only handler with real subprocess pipeline: write S2T rows (sql empty per Rule 1) -> create .sql files -> dbt seed -> dbt run --select -> dbt test --select -> end_of_task.py. Uses st.status with live step updates. 5-minute timeout per step. On any failure: rollback S2T rows + delete .sql files. Debug log written to deploy_debug.log.
what_happened: Tested end-to-end: success path ~110s with all steps visible. Rollback triggers correctly when dbt encounters broken ref(). Does not touch business_glossary.status (Rule 22).
conclusion: Deploy button is a real pipeline not a file-only shortcut. Deployment takes 1-2 minutes which is expected for dbt compilation.

hypothesis: sync_s2t_from_dbt.py now enforces full Rule 14 — deletes orphan S2T rows and inserts placeholders for new dbt output columns
what_we_did: S2T mapping had rows with target_column values not in the dbt model output (e.g. purchase_order_number in S2T but model aggregates to accuracy_percentage). sync_s2t_from_dbt.py only updated SQL for matching columns but left orphans untouched. Streamlit showed no SQL captured / no lineage for mismatched columns.
what_happened: Added Rule 14 enforcement: (1) delete S2T rows where target_column not in dbt output (2) insert placeholder rows for new model output columns with empty business_rule and notes=needs business rule authorship (3) log all changes to s2t_sync_warnings.log. Scoped to models where orphans were found to avoid placeholder explosion.
conclusion: sync_s2t_from_dbt.py fully enforces Rule 14: dbt model output columns are authoritative. S2T mapping rows auto-align on every end_of_task run. Orphan target_columns are deleted not silently ignored.

hypothesis: Column lineage scanner traces CTE expressions and records transformation_chain
what_we_did: _resolve_cte_column() now returns 4-tuple (origin_table origin_column expression chain). Expression = outermost non-trivial (for classification). Chain = all non-trivial expressions deepest→outermost (for lineage visualization). Re-classification only overrides 'direct' — non-direct outer expressions preserved. 15 columns improved from direct to correct type. Zero regressions vs original baseline.
what_happened: Added transformation_chain and transformation_chain_plain columns to dbt_column_lineage.csv. 34 columns have chains with LLM-generated plain-language labels. Outer SELECT expression appended as final chain step when non-trivial (18 multi-step chains).
conclusion: Scanner CTE resolution: origin from deepest level + expression from outermost non-trivial level + chain of all non-trivial levels. Classification only overrides direct. Chain enables multi-step lineage visualization.

hypothesis: Separate business-audience descriptions for Term Detail tab (Rule 7 enforcement)
what_we_did: Term Detail showed technical descriptions with SAP field names and pipeline jargon. Added transformation_logic_plain_business column to s2t_mapping.csv + business_join_description and business_filter_description columns to business_glossary.csv. New BUSINESS_SYSTEM_PROMPT forbids table names field names and technical terms. LLM generates per-term scoped join/filter descriptions from compiled SQL + column list.
what_happened: 40 business descriptions generated for all S2T rows. 24 term-scoped join/filter descriptions generated. Term Detail now shows pure business language. S2T Specification keeps technical descriptions unchanged.
conclusion: Rule 7 fully enforced: Term Detail = business audience (no SQL no table names). S2T Specification = technical audience (full SQL field names). Both derive from same dbt code via different LLM prompts.

hypothesis: Added Wiki Pages browser to Streamlit app under Documentation section
what_we_did: knowledge/ directory had 53 auto-generated markdown files but no way to browse them in the app. Created app/pages/Wiki_Pages.py with 8 category tabs + left panel page list with search + right panel styled markdown content. Added to navigation as Documentation > Wiki Pages.
what_happened: Wiki Pages reads from knowledge/ subdirectories. Custom dark-theme CSS for headings tables blockquotes code. Category tabs with page counts. Lazy-loaded with @st.cache_data(ttl=300). Saved domain reports appear automatically in Domain tab.
conclusion: Auto-generated wiki is browsable in-app. No editing — wiki regenerates from seeds. knowledge/domain/ reports from Domain Report tab appear in Wiki Pages automatically.

hypothesis: DQ tab now executes tests instead of hardcoding green checkmarks
what_we_did: DQ tab showed hardcoded checkmark icon for every test without running it. Term Detail correctly executed tests showing real PASS/FAIL. Created shared execute_dq_test() function used by both pages. DQ tab now shows summary bar + per-test PASS/FAIL with violation counts.
what_happened: Both pages agree on all test results. 146 violations correctly shown for assert_po_value_not_exceeding_50000 on both tabs. No more hardcoded icons anywhere.
conclusion: DQ results must always come from execute_dq_test() — never hardcoded icons. Both Term Detail and DQ tab use the same shared function and same Parquet-backed connection.

hypothesis: Domain Report tab persists LLM narrative to knowledge/domain/ markdown + domain_reports.csv with staleness detection
what_we_did: Domain Report was session-only — lost on page reload. Rewrote tab: saves narrative to knowledge/domain/{domain}_report.md with YAML frontmatter + metadata to domain_reports.csv seed. Staleness detection via findings_hash + count comparison. Removed Live Summary section — single LLM narrative only.
what_happened: Report persists across sessions. Staleness indicator shows current/stale/no-report states. Saved reports appear in Wiki Pages Domain tab automatically. Regeneration is on-demand only — never automatic.
conclusion: Domain Reports are persisted markdown files + CSV metadata. Staleness = hash(findings+QA) comparison. No automatic regeneration — user clicks Refresh when ready.

hypothesis: S2T Specification shows compiled SQL per layer with ref tracing
what_we_did: Added S2T Full SQL section with one collapsible expander per layer (Staging/Data Vault/Dimensional Model). Traces model refs backwards through layers via dbt_model_catalog. Each expander shows compiled SQL for all models in that layer. Also added Sample Data expander on Term Detail tab.
what_happened: S2T Full SQL traces fact_purchase_orders through 7 staging + 11 vault + 1 mart models. Compiled SQL read from dbt/target/compiled/ with all refs resolved to actual table names.
conclusion: S2T Full SQL shows compiled SQL per layer — Staging Layer + Data Vault Layer + Dimensional Model Layer. Collapsed by default. Traces refs backwards via dbt_model_catalog.

hypothesis: LLM prompt explicitly forbids pass-through language when SQL has functions
what_we_did: po_date description said straight pass-through but SQL was CAST(BEDAT AS DATE). Updated SYSTEM_PROMPT in sync_s2t_plain_from_dbt.py: if SQL contains ANY function (CASE CAST SUBSTR etc) NEVER say pass-through direct unchanged. Describe what the function does.
what_happened: 1 contradiction found and fixed. po_date now says Converts the purchase order creation date from SAP internal format to a standard date. All other 39 descriptions verified correct.
conclusion: LLM prompt must explicitly forbid pass-through language when SQL contains functions. scanner classification and LLM description are separate concerns — both must be accurate independently.

hypothesis: Structural cross-term observations need their own grain — collapsing into analysis_findings breaks the term-anchored invariant.
what_we_did: Created dbt/seeds/domain_facts.csv with 18 columns (fact_id PK DF-NNNN, category enum, scope_layer enum, scope_tables, fact_plain, fact_technical, evidence_sql, evidence_result_json, evidence_result_summary, evidence_refreshed_at, discovered_at, discovered_by, confidence, stale_after_days, auto_inject, priority_score, status, superseded_by). Added dbt/seeds/schema.yml with accepted_values tests on category/scope_layer/discovered_by/confidence/status and a dbt_utils.expression_is_true test asserting NOT (auto_inject AND status != active).
what_happened: New seed in place, empty by design. Guided Analysis — Domain tab is the only writer.
conclusion: analysis_findings is per-term per-query; domain_facts is structural cross-term observations. Never collapse the two.

hypothesis: A separate planner function would diverge over time; same function with a system_prompt override keeps parity.
what_we_did: Extended run_data_analysis() in app/claude_api.py with optional system_prompt_override and user_prompt_prefix parameters. Guided Analysis — Domain tab passes DOMAIN_SYSTEM_PROMPT (structural-observation framing) and a dedupe preamble from load_domain_context(). Guided Analysis — Business Term tab unchanged.
what_happened: Single planner codepath. Two tabs two framings zero forking.
conclusion: Same run_data_analysis function different system prompt. Never fork the planner.

hypothesis: Injecting domain_facts into every LLM call burns tokens without proportional benefit — especially in cache-warm batch scripts.
what_we_did: Created scripts/_domain_context_loader.py (reads data/parquet/main_seeds/domain_facts.parquet filters status=active plus auto_inject priority_score DESC truncation by token budget). Wired into exactly three sites: (a) Business_Glossary.py S2T call (800 tokens auto_inject=true) (b) Data_Analysis.py Domain Report generation (1500 tokens auto_inject=true) (c) Data_Analysis.py Guided-Domain planner (500 tokens auto_inject=false so drafts also dedupe). sync_s2t_plain_from_dbt.py intentionally skipped — 138 cached entries with roughly 69K tokens for near-zero benefit.
what_happened: Three injection sites live. Cache key for .s2t_plain_cache.json unchanged. Non-injected 6 claude_api.py call sites unchanged.
conclusion: Targeted injection only. sync_s2t_plain_from_dbt.py is intentionally excluded due to cache-cost vs. benefit tradeoff.

hypothesis: An auto-approved flag set by code is worthless governance theatre — Rule 22 restated for domain_facts.
what_we_did: Guided-Domain Promote-to-Fact button writes rows with status=draft regardless of the auto_inject checkbox. The curate section does not offer an inline status change — points users to edit the CSV manually. The schema.yml expression_is_true test enforces that auto_inject can only be true when status=active ensuring that until a human changes status the fact stays invisible to downstream injection.
what_happened: Guided-Domain produces drafts only. Human curation is the only path to active status.
conclusion: Rule 22 extended: domain_facts.status changes are manual only. Approval is never set by code even as a convenience.

hypothesis: An earlier decision excluded Guided-BT from domain_facts injection on token-budget grounds. That framing optimises minimum tokens instead of value-per-token. Result: every BT analysis re-discovers domain trivialities (currency distribution plant split material prefixes) in its query plan. One redundant query roundtrip costs more than 500 extra prompt tokens.
what_we_did: Extended run_data_analysis() with a mode-agnostic domain_context param. BT tab now calls load_domain_context with scope_tables inferred from s2t_mapping (business_glossary has no source-tables column) max_tokens=500 require_auto_inject=True. Four injection points now: S2T (800 auto_inject=true) Guided-BT (500 scope-filtered auto_inject=true) Guided-Domain (500 scope=None auto_inject=false so drafts dedupe) Domain Report (1500 scope=None auto_inject=true).
what_happened: Single planner codepath accepts domain_context string. Callers (BT tab Domain tab) compute their own context and pass it in. Planner is mode-agnostic.
conclusion: Injection is scope-filtered not content-filtered. Goal is relevant context not minimum context. Five other claude_api.py call sites and sync_s2t_plain_from_dbt.py remain excluded due to narrow scope or cache economics.

hypothesis: Branching on caller mode inside the shared planner couples the planner to its callers and makes testing harder.
what_we_did: run_data_analysis accepts three optional params (system_prompt_override user_prompt_prefix domain_context) but never checks which caller invoked it. Each caller decides how much context to build and via which scope filter. BT computes scope from s2t_mapping rows for this term_id; Domain uses scope_tables=None.
what_happened: Two tabs two framings one planner. No forks no flags. Adding a fifth caller in future requires zero changes inside run_data_analysis.
conclusion: The shared planner is dumb about modes. Callers own their context. Never branch inside run_data_analysis based on caller identity.

hypothesis: @st.cache_resource pins a single in-memory DuckDB connection for the whole Streamlit worker lifetime. Parquet view registration happens once at connect time. Any parquet file written by CLI tools (dbt seed end_of_task.py manual export) after Streamlit boots is invisible to the cached connection until server restart.
what_we_did: In app/db.py extended _register_parquet_views() to track view_name to filepath via a module-level dict and DROP VIEW IF EXISTS when the backing parquet is removed. Added _parquet_dir_latest_mtime() helper. query() now calls _refresh_views(conn) before each execute — mtime-gated so steady state is one rglob plus a max comparison (microseconds). A re-scan only fires when the directory mtime advances. Logs first scan and any non-trivial re-scan (added or removed views); silent otherwise.
what_happened: 8/8 verification checks pass. V1 fresh connection serves domain_facts cleanly. V2 smoke parquet added at runtime becomes visible on next query call (+1 logged). V3 smoke parquet deleted drops the view cleanly (-1 logged) with no uncaught exception reaching the user. V4 steady-state 10 sequential queries avg 31.7ms well under the 50ms target.
conclusion: Streamlit's cached DuckDB connection must mtime-gate a re-scan of data/parquet/ on every query. CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW for additions DROP VIEW IF EXISTS for removals both idempotent. Catches every future seed/mart automatically without server restart.

hypothesis: The first draft of the domain-facts UI inherited business_glossary governance (18-field form approval gate draft vs active toggle auto_inject checkbox). domain_facts are internal LLM context not business-approved deliverables. Rule 22 applies to business_glossary.status only.
what_we_did: Ripped out the Promote button 18-field form and Save Draft button. Added interpret_domain_fact() LLM helper in app/claude_api.py which produces the fact JSON. After each Guided-Domain query execution the tab auto-calls the helper auto-appends a row to dbt/seeds/domain_facts.csv with status=active auto_inject=true discovered_by=guided_domain_llm then syncs to DuckDB and Parquet. UI renders a compact fact card with exactly one button: Delete this fact. Curate section simplified to priority number input stale_after_days dropdown delete button re-run evidence — no status or auto_inject UI. JSON parse failure falls back to a minimal card with confidence=low and fact_plain flag.
what_happened: Zero-click capture. One-click delete. Curate for tweaks. Approval ceremony removed.
conclusion: Domain facts are internal LLM context — capture and delete only. No approval flow. Rule 22 governance is specific to business_glossary.status and does not generalise.

hypothesis: app/db.py query() was returning pd.DataFrame() on any exception — DuckDB catalog errors, syntax errors, IO errors. Downstream code dereferenced columns on the empty DataFrame and failed far from the source with a KeyError that hid the real problem. Silent fallbacks in a data-layer function amplify bugs rather than containing them.
what_we_did: Removed the blanket except Exception fallback in query(). DuckDB CatalogException and friends now propagate to the caller. Callers that genuinely tolerate empty results already wrap their query() calls in try/except and fall through to pd.DataFrame() themselves — grep confirmed the wrapped pattern is the norm for tolerant sites (Data_Analysis.py lines 85 90 103 502 556 1017 1047 1190 and similar in Business_Glossary.py). Bare callers dereference columns so a loud raise is the correct behaviour for them.
what_happened: A verification harness confirmed a missing-table query now raises DuckDB CatalogException. Wrapped callers unchanged in end-state behaviour (their except clause now catches the propagated error; before it never fired because query was silent).
conclusion: Data-layer functions must not swallow exceptions. Either succeed with real data or raise. Callers decide tolerance locally. See RULE 30.

hypothesis: Rule 27's _refresh_views fixed the view catalog but st.cache_data on query() is an independent cache. Pre-refresh query results (successful against stale catalog or empty-DF from transient errors) stayed cached for up to 60 seconds after the refresh fixed the catalog. The user saw multiple Catalog Error boxes on the BT tab because each module-level query() call had its own cached stale result.
what_we_did: In _refresh_views when _register_parquet_views reports added greater than 0 or removed greater than 0 also call query.clear() to flush the result cache. query is a module symbol resolved at call time so the forward reference works. Falls back to st.cache_data.clear() if query.clear is unreachable. The log line now includes cache cleared so the refresh event is visible.
what_happened: The verification harness confirms clear is invoked on both view-add and view-drop. Log shows parquet re-scan +1 views -0 views mtime now X query cache cleared.
conclusion: View catalog and result cache are two halves of one problem. When underlying parquet changes both must invalidate together. One without the other leaves a window where stale results propagate. Pair this with Rule 27 and Rule 30.

hypothesis: A dbt seed tracks ingestion events rather than a meta table in DuckDB or a JSON manifest on disk. Git-tracked auditable flows through the same Rule 27 Rule 30 view refresh and query cache discipline the rest of the project already uses. Zero new infra.
what_we_did: Created dbt/seeds/ingestion_log.csv (run_id started_at_utc finished_at_utc source_type row_count_total tables_touched trigger_user notes). Added schema.yml entry with unique+not_null on run_id accepted_values on source_type and an expression_is_true test enforcing started_at_utc <= finished_at_utc. Generator now appends a row on every run with id ING-YYYYMMDD-NNN computed from the existing rows for today.
what_happened: Freshness banner queries MAX(finished_at_utc) via the standard query() layer. Scanner re-seed list in end_of_task.py includes ingestion_log so new runs land in main_seeds automatically.
conclusion: ingestion_log is the single source of truth for last-raw-load time. Not a meta table not a manifest file. Future real-SAP loaders append the same schema with source_type=real_batch.

hypothesis: Green yellow red thresholds belong close to the gate logic initially. Until there is tuning demand externalising to dbt_project.yml or env vars adds plumbing without value.
what_we_did: Hardcoded GREEN_MAX_DAYS=3 and YELLOW_MAX_DAYS=14 in app/freshness.py. gap_days = (last_ingestion_utc - min_evidence_refreshed_utc).days clamped at zero. <=3 green <=14 yellow otherwise red. Comparisons only consider active auto_inject=true facts with non-null stale_after_days.
what_happened: One place to change when tuning demand emerges. Single source of truth for the gate math.
conclusion: Thresholds stay in app/freshness.py until a real reason to config-ify them appears. Move later not now.

hypothesis: BT planner consumes domain facts to avoid re-proposing queries. If facts are stale the planner should still see them with a warning. Blocking BT prevents the user from even exploring the analysis that would reveal the drift. Stale knowledge beats no knowledge for read-path analyses.
what_we_did: In Data_Analysis.py BT tab L465 render_freshness_banner('bt') renders but the Plan button stays enabled. Guided-Domain Plan Domain Report Generate and S2T Create all receive disabled=is_write_blocked(). BT is the only of the four that does not gate.
what_happened: BT tab usable even when ingestion is old. Write-path buttons disable with help= tooltip Domain facts are stale re-run ingestion and refresh.
conclusion: BT warns never blocks. Write-path actions block on red. The four-site split is deliberate and documented.

hypothesis: Data ingestion is CLI only. The generator is scripts/generate_sap_sample_data.py and runs in a terminal. The red banner instructs the user to run it manually. Domain facts refresh is different — it is knowledge recompute against existing raw data — so Streamlit does shell out to scripts/refresh_domain_facts.py via subprocess on the yellow state.
what_we_did: Freshness banner on yellow exposes Refresh domain facts now button. On red no button is shown because refresh cannot fix stale data that ingestion has not caught up with. On no_baseline the message reads Run python scripts/generate_sap_sample_data.py in a terminal.
what_happened: Narrow exception for knowledge refresh only. The broader principle — ingestion pipelines do not live inside Streamlit — is preserved.
conclusion: Streamlit triggers knowledge recompute never data ingestion. If that distinction blurs in future revisit the exception.

hypothesis: Numeric thresholds alone miss structural changes (a new movement type appearing). Set-expansion alone misses magnitude shifts (EUR share drops from 94% to 70% but no new currency). Use both: new distinct value on the primary grouping column OR dominant-row percentage shift >5pp.
what_we_did: scripts/refresh_domain_facts.py classify_drift: compares record lists element-by-element for no_drift. If not identical extracts the primary grouping column (first non-numeric text column) and checks set expansion. If that fails checks for a pct column and measures shift against MATERIAL_PCT_SHIFT=5.0. Otherwise defaults to noise so numbers get refreshed without the supersede cost.
what_happened: no_drift -> timestamp refresh only (0 tokens). noise -> focused LLM call rewriting fact_technical only (~400 tokens). material -> supersede old fact + new fact via interpret_domain_fact (~1100 tokens). unparseable -> log and skip.
conclusion: Hybrid rule. Set expansion is the hard rule for new values. 5pp is the soft threshold for magnitude. Both apply. Noise is the fallback bucket.

hypothesis: User-authored artefacts (business terms, S2T mappings, dashboards) should be archived not hard-deleted. Deletion erases the audit trail; archive preserves it and lets us learn from prior attempts. The existing status column (draft approved) collapses to (draft approved archived) with archive_id archived_at_utc archived_reason_code archived_reason_text columns added alongside.
what_we_did: Chosen design: collapse archive into the existing status column. Extended dbt/seeds/business_glossary.csv with 4 archive columns. Added accepted_values test on status (draft approved archived). Added custom singular test assert_business_glossary_term_name_unique_when_not_archived.sql enforcing composite uniqueness on term_name for non-archived rows — allows a new active term to reuse a name whose prior instance is archived. New seed dbt/seeds/archive_log.csv captures one row per archive event with archive_id business_term_id term_name archived_at_utc reason code reason text learning_signal archived_by s2t_row_ids target_models files_archived. app/archive_term.py run_archive() mutates business_glossary status directly — this is the explicit exception to Rule 22 and is only invoked via a user click on the archive confirm button.
what_happened: Archive expander added to S2T tab in Business_Glossary.py visible only when term has at least one s2t_mapping row and status is not already archived. Draft-can-be-archived: no approval gate before archive. Active dropdowns filter archived terms via _active_glossary slice.
conclusion: Archive preserves every downstream artefact (s2t_mapping analysis_findings dbt_column_lineage). Caveat: after archive the prior status draft vs approved is NOT recoverable from business_glossary alone — archive_reason_code (wrong_grain bad_definition redefined obsolete other) is the primary audit signal for MVP. If the distinction matters later archive_log can be extended with a pre_archive_status column.

hypothesis: Not every archive event should feed future LLM calls. Demo rehearsals test runs and reproducible-state scenarios need archives that leave no trace in future prompts. A boolean learning_signal flag decouples was-this-archived from should-this-influence-future-work.
what_we_did: archive_log.csv carries a learning_signal column default TRUE. app/_archive_context_loader.py load_archived_term_context(term_name) filters main_seeds.archive_log on term_name match plus learning_signal=TRUE sorts by archived_at_utc DESC takes top 3 and formats a ## Previously archived attempts block under 500 tokens. The block is injected into create_s2t_with_implementation via a new archived_context parameter placed before domain_context so prior failures frame the rest of the prompt.
what_happened: LLM filter proven programmatically: seed 2 archives for the same term_name one with learning_signal=TRUE and one FALSE. Loader returns only the TRUE one. The FALSE entry is invisible to the LLM exactly as designed.
conclusion: Default to learning_signal=TRUE. Users actively uncheck for the non-learning case. The flag belongs in the archive_log not in business_glossary because it is a property of the archive event not the term.

hypothesis: Archived dbt model files must stop being compiled by dbt but must NOT be deleted. Moving them to dbt/models/archive/<archive_id>/ preserves them on disk and excluding that subfolder via dbt_project.yml keeps the active workspace uncluttered while keeping the audit trail fully browsable.
what_we_did: run_archive() in app/archive_term.py uses shutil.move (handles Windows cross-drive cases) to relocate dbt/models/<layer>/<name>.sql to dbt/models/archive/<archive_id>/<layer>/<name>.sql preserving layer subdir. dbt_project.yml models section adds archive: +enabled: false under cpe_procurement_analytics. Missing files are warnings not fatal. PermissionError is caught and reported. The scanner in end_of_task.py naturally drops catalog entries for the moved files on the next run.
what_happened: dbt compile skips the archive/ subfolder. The scanner does not re-add archived models to dbt_column_lineage / dbt_model_catalog / dbt_model_relationships. Active dbt workspace stays clean.
conclusion: Archive = move not delete. Active dbt/models stays uncluttered. dbt_project.yml archive: +enabled: false is the single exclusion point. The scanner handles the catalog cleanup automatically.

hypothesis: Windows core.autocrlf=true converts LF to CRLF on checkout and back to LF on commit. For dbt seed CSVs that DuckDB parses via its CSV sniffer this turns into a silent CRLF introduction vector every time git checkout merge or rebase touches a seed. A .gitattributes file at repo root forces LF regardless of local git config.
what_we_did: Created .gitattributes at project root with four rules: dbt/seeds/*.csv text eol=lf ; dbt/tests/*.sql text eol=lf ; dbt/models/**/*.sql text eol=lf ; *.py text eol=lf. After creation ran git add --renormalize . to rewrite every currently-tracked file under those patterns so the working tree matches the new normalization contract.
what_happened: Silent CRLF corruption from Windows autocrlf is no longer possible for seeds SQL files or Python files regardless of developer OS. Git operations normalise on their own.
conclusion: Layer 1 of RULE 34. Zero Python code churn. Defends against every git-path CRLF introduction vector across every developer machine.

hypothesis: Even with .gitattributes and correct Python writers a future writer might skip the lineterminator convention or an external tool like Excel or a copy-paste edit might introduce CRLF between git operations. The window between the bad write and the next git checkout is enough for a dbt seed to fail.
what_we_did: Added a narrow hook in scripts/end_of_task.py just before the scanner-seed block: iterate dbt/seeds/*.csv read bytes if b CRLF in raw write back with CRLF replaced by LF. Logs which files were normalised. Runs once per end_of_task invocation before any dbt seed fires.
what_happened: Layer 3 of RULE 34. Defense in depth. Any writer that slips past Layer 2 convention and any external tool that edited the CSV gets caught automatically. Zero false positives because only files that actually contain CRLF get rewritten.
conclusion: end_of_task.py pre-dbt-seed LF normalisation is the last-mile safety net. Cheap one-file-stat per seed per pipeline invocation. Never rewrites files that are already clean.

hypothesis: Path.write_text on Windows opens the file in text mode which converts every standalone LF in the Python string into CRLF in the written bytes. For files that will be parsed by line-ending-sensitive tools this introduces bugs that are invisible to Linux testers and reproduce only on Windows.
what_we_did: Fixed the archive verification harness where its finally{} restore was using Path.write_text to restore seed CSV contents captured by Path.read_text. Replaced with Path.write_bytes(str.encode utf-8). Grep confirmed no other write_text call in the codebase targets a seed CSV — the other write_text sites write markdown JSON and log files that are not CSV-sniffed.
what_happened: The verify harness no longer reintroduces CRLF during seed restoration. The archive_log.csv that archive_term writes with LF stays LF end to end. Root cause of hours of CSV sniffer debugging is resolved in one line change.
conclusion: For any file that needs deterministic line endings use Path.write_bytes(str.encode utf-8) or open(path, w, encoding=utf-8, newline=). Reserve Path.write_text only for markdown JSON and logs that no line-ending-sensitive tool reads.

hypothesis: Harness assertions that string-match dbt CLI output are brittle because dbt's stdout wording changes between versions. One verification-harness check (V9) asserted phrases like 0 of 0 and no nodes selected which were valid in older dbt but dbt 1.11.8 emits does not match any enabled nodes plus Nothing to do instead. The behaviour was correct; only the phrasing moved.
what_we_did: Expanded the V9 allow-list to accept five phrases covering multiple dbt versions: no nodes matching, 0 of 0, no nodes selected, does not match any enabled nodes, nothing to do. Also tightened the check to require exit code 0 AND any of those phrases — testing the structural outcome instead of a single exact string.
what_happened: V9 now passes on dbt 1.11.8 and will keep passing on future patch versions whose stdout wording drifts. Full V1-V11 harness now 27/27 green.
conclusion: When testing dbt CLI output always match the structural outcome (exit code plus presence of one of a set of phrases) rather than a single exact string. dbt stdout is user-facing text and legitimately rewords between versions.

hypothesis: pandas NA (extension-string or extension-int dtype) raises TypeError boolean value of NA is ambiguous when evaluated in any boolean context including the right-hand side of x or y. The project had ~28 sites using the dict-like get default or fallback pattern on pandas rows which is a landmine whenever a real CSV cell is NULL.
what_we_did: Project-wide grep of app/ for .get(col, default) or fallback when the variable name is a known pandas-row container (term_row s2t_row fact_row f_row cl_row _saved_meta dv _r r row term prog contract zt cr ent _rel_seed or anything from iterrows or iloc[0]). Replaced 28 sites with the safe pattern raw = r.get(col, default); x = str(raw).strip() if pd.notna(raw) else default. Left untouched ~30 sites on LLM JSON response dicts and csv.DictReader dicts which cannot produce pd.NA. Added pandas import to app/_archive_context_loader.py which previously did not have one.
what_happened: Grep confirms zero remaining .get() or sites on pandas-row-named variables. Syntax clean on all 6 touched files. Streamlit running process does not need restart — Streamlit auto-reloads Python source on file change. Business Glossary tabs render without the TypeError that surfaced mid-demo-rehearsal.
conclusion: Never use x or fallback on a value that came from a pandas row. Use pd.notna guards or a dedicated helper. LLM JSON response dicts and csv.DictReader dicts are safe because they do not produce pd.NA. Classify before fixing to avoid churn on already-safe sites.

hypothesis: Streamlit's file watcher re-imports db.py on any source byte change (git renormalize / a hotfix edit / etc) which resets _registered_views dict and _last_view_scan_mtime to empty while @st.cache_resource may or may not preserve the underlying DuckDB connection across that reload. If connection persists but module state resets the mtime gate short-circuits incorrectly (latest <= 0.0 is False so it proceeds but the dict doesn't know about views). If connection is replaced but module state claimed it knew views the inverse drift happens.
what_we_did: Added a drift-detection block at the top of _refresh_views in app/db.py. Probes the connection's information_schema.tables for views in any main_% schema via SELECT 1 LIMIT 1. Compares against bool(_registered_views). On mismatch in either direction (module empty plus conn has views OR module non-empty plus conn has none) force _last_view_scan_mtime=0.0 and log a drift-detected line. The existing mtime-gated path then runs a full _register_parquet_views scan and query.clear() to resync both halves. Wrapped the probe in try/except so a closed or broken connection still falls through to a full scan.
what_happened: V1 normal-flow test: single initial scan on first query second query skips re-scan (steady state preserved). V3 drift-simulation test: clear _registered_views and _last_view_scan_mtime manually then query again. Log shows drift detected line plus full re-scan plus query cache cleared plus query succeeds returning 25 rows. Module state rebuilt: 161 views registered. RULE 30 cache-clear still fires in the added greater-than-zero branch.
conclusion: RULE 27 extension 2026-04-18: Streamlit module-reload plus @st.cache_resource connection persistence produces a narrow drift window. Detect it at the top of _refresh_views via information_schema probe and force re-scan. Defensive and cheap one SELECT 1 per query call. Closes the recurring catalog does not exist error that kept resurfacing.

hypothesis: pandas.Timestamp is a subclass of datetime.datetime so isinstance(ts, datetime) returns True but its tz-manipulation methods delegate to pandas-specific implementations. ts.replace(tzinfo=X) does not behave like datetime.replace(tzinfo=X) — it calls tz_convert internally expecting different argument counts which raises TypeError tz_convert takes exactly 2 positional arguments (1 given). Data read from parquet arrives as Timestamp data from datetime.now arrives as datetime. Mixed code paths are the trap.
what_we_did: Fixed app/freshness.py. _fmt_local at L175-185 now normalises pd.Timestamp to python datetime via to_pydatetime() before attaching UTC tzinfo and calling astimezone. _to_utc_naive at L43-54 now also normalises Timestamp to datetime at the top of its isinstance branch so the subsequent astimezone plus replace(tzinfo=None) chain stays in stdlib-datetime semantics. Both functions now handle None NaT Timestamp and datetime inputs uniformly.
what_happened: V4 probe confirmed: _fmt_local returns never for None and NaT; renders Timestamp naive Timestamp with tz and datetime all with the same local-timezone string. _to_utc_naive returns proper naive-UTC datetime for each input type and None for NaT. compute_freshness_state runs without TypeError.
conclusion: For any function that may receive either pandas.Timestamp or datetime.datetime normalise to datetime via to_pydatetime() before attaching tzinfo. Alternative: stay in pandas land via Timestamp.tz_localize(X) and Timestamp.tz_convert(Y). Never chain .replace(tzinfo=X).astimezone() on a value that might be a Timestamp. See RULE 36.

hypothesis: close_connection() in app/db.py was the authoritative drop-all-state signal but cleared only two of four state locations: get_connection @st.cache_resource plus st.cache_data on query. It left _registered_views dict and _last_view_scan_mtime untouched. After a Deploy button run which calls close_connection() at the end the next get_connection() built a fresh DuckDB connection but _register_parquet_views saw _registered_views already claimed all 161 paths and skipped every CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW. Fresh connection had zero views. Module state claimed 161. Queries failed with Catalog Error until Hotfix 2 drift-detection rescued them on the next call — but that rescue fires on every Deploy because the drift is being introduced at the signal source not repaired.
what_we_did: Added two lines at the top of close_connection() in app/db.py before the existing two cache clears. _registered_views.clear() plus _last_view_scan_mtime = 0.0 and declared both as global. Four state locations now clear together on one signal. Hotfix 2 drift-detection stays in _refresh_views for the other trigger class (Streamlit file watcher re-imports db.py independently of close_connection). Two safety nets for two scenarios.
what_happened: V1 live probe: primed state 26 rows plus 162 registered views plus mtime. Called close_connection. State zeroed: views=0 plus mtime=0.0. Next query rebuilt cleanly 26 rows plus 162 views plus mtime refreshed. V2 ast.parse: OK. V3 live Streamlit test handed off to user to click Deploy plus Term Detail and confirm no catalog error.
conclusion: close_connection() is the authoritative state-reset signal. It clears all four state locations: get_connection cache st.cache_data _registered_views _last_view_scan_mtime. Fix at the signal source not via downstream detection. RULE 27 extension 2026-04-18 #2 codifies this invariant.

hypothesis: s2t_mapping.csv was wiped to header-only mid-session and forensics could not identify the exact code path that truncated the file. The verify harness that was the most plausible culprit had been deleted but we had no guarantee another pd.read_csv-mutate-pd.to_csv pattern anywhere in the codebase could not repeat the same truncation.
what_we_did: Added app/_csv_safeguard.py exposing assert_csv_safe(path df) plus assert_csv_safe_row_count(path n) helpers keyed off SAFEGUARDED_SEEDS with s2t_mapping rule min_rows_absolute=30 max_delete_per_op=10. Wired into three s2t_mapping writers: Business_Glossary.py Deploy step a (csv.DictWriter append) uses the count variant after the append loop. Business_Glossary.py rollback (pd.read_csv-iloc-to_csv) uses the df variant before to_csv. scripts/sync_s2t_from_dbt.py adjusts sys.path to import from app/ and calls the df variant before save_csv(s2t_mapping).
what_happened: V1 Deploy adds 5 rows 33-to-38 passes. V2 rollback 38-to-33 delta=5 passes. V3 Rule 14 minus-4-plus-6 net plus-2 passes. V4 0-row write raises min_rows message. V5 20-row write on 38-row baseline raises (floor catches first since 20 less than 30). All five verifications green. No regression to existing Deploy or Build-S2T flows.
conclusion: Guard at the write boundary not at every suspect pandas roundtrip. One helper per seed rule dict. When the safeguard raises never bypass via try/except — fix the root cause either in the writer or by widening the rule bounds for a new legitimate operation. See RULE 37.

hypothesis: csv.DictWriter opens the file in "w" mode (truncates to 0 bytes) BEFORE validating row dict keys against the declared fieldnames. If any row carries an extra key, DictWriter raises ValueError mid-write — AFTER the file has been truncated and the header line written. Result: corrupted header-only CSV (205 bytes on a 13-field schema). Root cause of intermittent s2t_mapping.csv wipes across a series of hotfixes; safeguard row-count check alone could not catch it because rows had 40 items at write time, passing min_rows_absolute=30 and max_delete_per_op=10.
what_we_did: Reproduced the wipe deterministically via an instrumented end_of_task replay. In sync_s2t_plain_from_dbt.py main(): fieldnames is captured at L357 from s2t[0].keys() (13 keys). A second loop at L445-494 adds transformation_logic_plain_business to each row dict. The save_csv call at L496 was passed the STALE 13-field fieldnames list — every row had the new 14th key. Traceback: csv.py line 220 _dict_to_list raises ValueError on row 0. By then path.open("w") at save_csv L68 had already truncated the file and writeheader() had written 205 bytes. Dual fix applied: (1) refresh fieldnames via dict.fromkeys at L496 covering all keys in current rows; (2) new assert_fieldnames_cover_rows in app/_csv_safeguard.py wired into save_csv helpers in both sync_s2t_from_dbt.py and sync_s2t_plain_from_dbt.py BEFORE the path.open("w"). Row-count check runs first (cheap common bug) then fieldnames check second (catches schema drift).
what_happened: V1 Fix-1-live: 14-column save succeeds 33-row preserved. V2 Fix-1-reverted: Fix-2 RAISES RuntimeError with precise message "Row 0 has keys not in fieldnames: [transformation_logic_plain_business]" BEFORE file truncation — file stays 33 rows on disk. V3 Fix-1-reapplied: V1 scenario reconfirm PASS. Bonus: row-count guard still fires on 0-row save. All three safeguards layered: row-count + fieldnames + rule14-placeholder-schema match.
conclusion: Never trust that a writer helper validates its inputs before mutating the output file. csv.DictWriter truncate-then-fail is the reference case but any "open for write then validate" pattern has the same trap. Move all validation BEFORE file.open("w"). For seeds with evolving schemas (new derived columns added by downstream LLM enrichment), refresh fieldnames from actual row keys at each write site rather than capturing once at function entry. See RULE 37 extension.

hypothesis: When dbt seed --full-refresh loads an empty header-only CSV (e.g. after a DictWriter truncate-trap or any wipe event), DuckDB's CSV sniffer has no data rows to infer types from and falls back to INTEGER for every column. That broken schema persists in cpe_analytics.duckdb — every subsequent non-refresh dbt seed fails with Conversion Error when trying to append VARCHAR data (e.g. "S001", "BG001") into the INTEGER-typed columns. Invisible collateral damage from any wipe event — can block re-Deploy even after the CSV itself is restored to a valid 33-row state.
what_we_did: Observed in the 08:00 re-Deploy of BG026 cpe_active_deployed_count. Step c (dbt seed --select s2t_mapping WITHOUT --full-refresh) failed with rc=1: Conversion Error on Line 2, "Could not convert string S001 to INTEGER". The CSV itself was valid (33 rows, VARCHAR data, LF-only) but DESCRIBE main_seeds.s2t_mapping showed all 13 columns typed INTEGER with 0 rows — the 02:39 end_of_task.py had --full-refresh-loaded the wiped header-only CSV and DuckDB inferred INTEGER. Deploy rollback correctly reverted Step a's 5 rows; git diff was empty. Fix: one-shot dbt seed --full-refresh --select s2t_mapping against the restored 33-row CSV, then export_parquet. VARCHAR re-inferred; ready for re-Deploy.
what_happened: After fix: DESCRIBE main_seeds.s2t_mapping shows (id VARCHAR) and 12 similar entries with all columns VARCHAR 33 rows. Parquet re-exported with correct types. dbt seed round-trip no longer fails on VARCHAR-into-INTEGER. No regression to other seeds — only s2t_mapping was affected because it was the only seed the wipe event truncated.
conclusion: After any wipe event that resulted in a --full-refresh against a header-only CSV, DO NOT try to re-populate via non-refresh dbt seed. Always run dbt seed --full-refresh --select <seed> once against the restored CSV before resuming normal operations. Consider adding a guard in end_of_task.py CRLF-normaliser loop: if a seed CSV has only a header line (no data rows), skip the subsequent --full-refresh for that seed so the prior valid schema stays in DuckDB. See also decision #57 csv_dictwriter_truncate_trap — this is the downstream collateral symptom.

hypothesis: LLM-generated dbt model SQL frequently references non-existent columns via plausible-but-wrong names — classic adjacent-column hallucinations like "material_description" instead of "model_description". Without auto-retry every such Deploy rolls back immediately and the user has to hand-edit SQL or fall back to Guided Analysis. Token cost is small (each repair prompt is shorter than the original create_s2t_with_implementation call); error messages are the cheapest teacher — pass them back instead of rolling back.
what_we_did: Added an auto-retry loop around Step d (dbt run) in app/pages/Business_Glossary.py. Max 3 attempts total. On each subprocess.CalledProcessError the loop: (1) parses stderr/stdout for Binder column-not-found patterns (Referenced column "X" not found + Candidate bindings: Y Z W + Failure in model M), (2) parses the .sql file on disk for {{ ref(name) }} calls, (3) dumps information_schema for each ref across main_staging/main_marts/main_obt/main_knowledge/main_vault/main_seeds, (4) calls new claude_api.repair_dbt_model_sql(model_filename, current_sql, dbt_error_text, schema_dump) which returns JSON with corrected SQL preserving jinja refs, (5) overwrites the .sql file and retries dbt run. Non-Binder errors (syntax, timeout, permission) skip the retry and go straight to rollback. After 3 failed attempts the rollback path fires with an enhanced fail_error message directing the user to Guided Analysis -- Business Term for deeper context before retrying.
what_happened: V1a parser: extracted col="material_description" candidates=["material_number","model_description","days_since_receipt","manufacturer","serial_number"] model="obt_active_cpe_count" from real 08:41 dbt error text. V1b schema dump: {{ ref("dim_equipment") }} + {{ ref("fact_goods_movements") }} both resolved in main_marts with full column lists including the correct model_description. V1c live API call: repair_dbt_model_sql returned {"sql": "WITH x AS (...) SELECT equipment_number, model_description FROM x"} — hallucinated column replaced with the correct candidate automatically. V2: simulated 3 persistent failures; retry loop capped at 3; rollback path triggered with Guided Analysis message. V3 deferred to user live Deploy.
conclusion: Any LLM-to-SQL-to-execution loop should pass failures back with (a) the raw error, (b) any candidate-bindings hint the engine offers, (c) actual information_schema of referenced tables. All three are needed — without schema context the LLM just guesses again at the same class of error. With them the LLM usually picks correctly on attempt 2. Cap retries at 3 and degrade gracefully to Guided Analysis for pathological cases. See RULE 38.

hypothesis: _run_subprocess timeout=300s (5 min) was too tight for Step f end_of_task when the deploy auto-retry triggers Rule 14 placeholder insertions. Those placeholders invalidate sync_s2t_plain cache entries (new IDs + new SQL hashes) so each gets a fresh LLM call, adding 60-90s to the aggregate. 09:02 Deploy TIMED OUT at exactly 300s despite retry and Step d/e succeeding.
what_we_did: Added optional timeout kwarg to _run_subprocess in Business_Glossary.py (default=300 preserves prior behavior for dbt seed/run/test). Step f call site now passes timeout=900 (15 min). V1 end_of_task standalone measured at 260.7s on warm-ish cache — 900s gives ~640s headroom for cold-cache retry scenarios. Updated the TimeoutExpired branch error text from "Timed out after 5 minutes" to "15 minutes" so user-facing failure messages match actual cap.
what_happened: V1 live probe: end_of_task.py rc=0 elapsed=260.7s on current (warm) cache state. No step timed out. WARNINGS emitted about wiki regen + context regen (unrelated). Only Step f got the timeout bump — dbt seed/run/test still bounded at 300s each so hung dbt jobs still fail fast. No regression to other subprocess call sites.
conclusion: Per-step timeouts: dbt operations get the baseline 300s; Step f end_of_task gets 900s because it chains ~6 sub-scripts with variable LLM call count. When deploy retries introduce new placeholder rows, cache-miss LLM regen is the dominant cost. Escalate future timeout increases only if end_of_task exceeds 900s on a DEMONSTRATED cold-cache baseline — otherwise fix the upstream churn.

hypothesis: Deploy _rollback() in Business_Glossary.py used df.iloc[:-s2t_rows_added] which assumes Step a appends are the LAST rows. Rule 14 enforcement inside end_of_task can insert placeholder rows AFTER Step a (observed: 09:02 Deploy left CSV at 36 rows instead of 33 after rollback — Rule 14 added 7 placeholders between Step a and rollback, and iloc[:-4] dropped the last 4 placeholders instead of Step a rows). Position-based rollback is brittle to any downstream write between append and rollback.
what_we_did: Added written_s2t_ids: list[str] tracked alongside s2t_rows_added. Step a appends now append each freshly-assigned S0xx ID into written_s2t_ids immediately after writer.writerow. _rollback() replaces df.iloc[:-s2t_rows_added] with df = df[~df["id"].astype(str).isin(written_s2t_ids)]. The logged message now reports both the count removed AND the tracked ID list so post-hoc forensics can verify which rows were targeted.
what_happened: V3 simulation: pre-Step-a 33 rows. Step a appends S034-S037 (tagged). Rule 14 drops S034+S035 as orphans and inserts 5 P-prefixed placeholders. Rollback-by-ID identifies remaining Step a IDs (S036, S037) and removes only those. Final state: 33 original rows intact, 0 Step a IDs remaining, 5 Rule 14 placeholders preserved (intentional — they are Rule 14 artifact, not Step a). PASS.
conclusion: Any rollback following an append operation that may be chased by other writes must track specific identifiers. iloc[:-N] is a position-based rollback and assumes single-writer invariant. When a multi-stage pipeline can modify the same table between append and rollback, swap to id-based predicates. See RULE 39.

hypothesis: Post-Hotfix-1 V3 test revealed: rollback-by-ID correctly removes Step a tagged IDs but leaves Rule 14 placeholder rows that piggy-backed on the same target_model. Those placeholders reference the .sql file that rollback also deletes — so next end_of_task scan sees orphan rows pointing to non-existent models. Not fatal (next scan flags + cleans them) but leaves CSV inconsistent between a failed Deploy and the next attempt.
what_we_did: Extended _rollback() in Business_Glossary.py. Compute deleted_model_names = {Path(fp).stem for fp in written_files if fp.endswith(".sql")}. Build keep-mask that drops rows matching EITHER Step a IDs (step_a_mask) OR (business_term_id == term_id AND target_model in deleted_model_names AND not already counted in step_a_mask). The term_id scope is a safer proxy than a was_new flag: it ensures rollback never touches rows belonging to a different term that happens to reference the same target_model name (e.g. an older term redeployed that also output to obt_active_cpe_count). Read CSV with keep_default_na=False dtype=str so the filter comparisons see the raw string values, not parsed NaN.
what_happened: V1 simulation: 33 base + 4 Step a (S038-S041 BG026 obt_active_cpe_count) + 3 Rule 14 (S042-S044 same BG026 same model) → 40 rows. Rollback with written_files=[obt_active_cpe_count.sql] and term_id=BG026 returned {removed_step_a: 4, removed_orphans: 3, after: 33}. PASS. V2 isolation: 33 base + 2 BG999 rows on same model + 2 BG026 Step a → 37 rows. Rollback scoped to BG026 returned {removed_step_a: 2, removed_orphans: 0, after: 35}. BG999 rows untouched. PASS. Isolation holds even when unrelated term happens to reference the same target_model name.
conclusion: Rollback must clean not just the primary append (tracked by IDs) but also any downstream artifacts that piggy-backed on the same resources — here: Rule 14 placeholders that reference the .sql files this rollback is deleting. Scope the downstream cleanup to the current term_id so unrelated rows are never touched. The safer_proxy approach (term_id scope) is chosen over a was_new flag on .sql files — it handles re-Deploy of an existing term correctly without extra tracking state. See RULE 39 extension #2.

hypothesis: SQL that compiles and runs is not proof it measures what the business term claims to measure. Example: cpe_active_deployed_count first Deploy produced a company-level single-row aggregate even though term.grain says "per serial number". dbt run, dbt test, and end_of_task all reported success. Numbers in the dashboard were wrong. No existing Deploy step catches semantic (as opposed to syntactic) mismatch. The deploy auto-retry handles only parseable Binder errors.
what_we_did: Added a semantic validation gate BETWEEN Step d (dbt run) and Step e (dbt test) in app/pages/Business_Glossary.py. Two new helpers in app/claude_api.py: validate_model_semantics(term_row, model_name, model_sql, row_count, column_types, sample_rows) returns {match, issues[], summary}, and repair_semantic_mismatch(term_row, model_sql, issues) returns {sql}. Validator checks three DETERMINISTIC dimensions only — grain / filter / unit. Definition drift intentionally skipped: subjective, overlaps, risks false positives. Conservative stance: only severity=critical blocks Deploy; warnings are informational. For each written .sql file, per-model loop: read SQL, query COUNT(*) + DESCRIBE + LIMIT 5 from main_<layer>.<model>, call validator. If critical: call repair, overwrite .sql, re-run dbt for that model, loop. Max 3 attempts total. Two fail modes feed into existing rollback: failed_step="semantic validation" (exhausted retries) with Guided-Analysis pointer message, OR failed_step="semantic repair" when a repaired SQL no longer compiles.
what_happened: V1 live API grain mismatch (term=per plant, SQL=COUNT(*) whole-company): match=False critical=1 grain_critical=1. V2 live API filter mismatch (term says exclude blocked suppliers, SQL lacks WHERE): match=False critical=1 filter_critical=1. V3 live API happy-path (cpe_active_deployed_count existing compiled SQL vs grain="per serial number"): match=False critical=1 grain — validator independently detected the exact same mismatch the user archived the term for. V4 retry-cap covered by code inspection: sem_attempt >= SEMANTIC_MAX_ATTEMPTS sets failed_step, fall-through to rollback; broken_by_repair arm handles the case where a repaired SQL no longer compiles. Token budget: happy path ~1500 tokens; worst case 3 validate + 2 repair ~8k tokens, cheaper than one Guided Analysis cycle.
conclusion: Semantic validation belongs inside the Deploy flow, not downstream. Compile-time green means nothing for business-correctness. When cost-bounding the validator: only deterministic dimensions that can be checked against declared term attributes (grain, filter, unit). Skip definition drift — it is subjective and overlaps with the other three. Bias toward false negatives (accepting ambiguous cases) since blocking a legitimate Deploy is much worse than letting one through. The live V3 test exposing the cpe_active_deployed_count grain bug validates the choice to ship this gate. See RULE 40.

hypothesis: Business Glossary term selector correctly excluded archived rows via an _active_glossary post-filter at L2071-2075, but Data Analysis Guided Analysis selector loaded the full glossary unfiltered and showed archived terms in its selectbox. Separately the Create-S2T prompt in Business_Glossary.py passed the full business_glossary CSV (including archived rows) to the Claude prompt — archived terms could confuse the LLM about whether a term exists and recycle stale definitions into new suggestions.
what_we_did: Data_Analysis.py L412-417 now post-filters: _active_glossary = glossary[glossary.get("status","active").astype(str) != "archived"]; term_pool derives from _active_glossary (preserving the existing domain filter via intersection). Business_Glossary.py L1231-1234 Create-S2T prompt query now has WHERE COALESCE(CAST(status AS VARCHAR), "") != "archived" so the glossary_text blob sent to Claude does not include archived rows.
what_happened: V1: glossary=27 rows archived=1 (BG026); _active=26; BG026 is in full but not in _active. V3: prompt glossary query returned 26 rows, zero of them status=archived. V4 cross-page audit: the only remaining unfiltered read is components/metric_card.py L15 which is a LOOKUP-BY-ID for tooltip display (archived lookup is legitimate audit, not a selector). No third bug.
conclusion: Every business_term selector AND every LLM prompt that includes glossary rows must filter WHERE status != "archived". Archived stays in table for audit but is invisible to operational UI and AI. Lookup-by-ID call sites (e.g. tooltip from metric_card) are exempt because they legitimately render data for any id passed in. See also decision #47 learning_signal filter and #58 empty_csv_corrupts_duckdb_schema_inference.

hypothesis: analysis_findings rows are written keyed by business_term_id (e.g. "BG026"). When a term is archived and the user creates a fresh term with the same term_name (which the glossary uniqueness constraint allows — WHERE status != archived), the new term got a NEW business_term_id (e.g. "BG039") and all prior Guided Analysis insights stored against the archived BG026 became invisible to the new term. Orphaned insights = forced re-analysis every archive cycle.
what_we_did: Read-time resolution in Data_Analysis.py L444-462 (the "Existing findings" block). Current term is resolved to its term_name; glossary is scanned for EVERY id that shares that term_name (active plus archived); analysis_findings is filtered with .isin(matching_term_ids). The write path at L862 still persists against the CURRENT term_id so the historical FK stays intact for audit. Read aggregates across the whole term_name family.
what_happened: V2 simulation: current glossary has term_name=cpe_active_deployed_count across two ids (BG026 archived, BG027 draft). analysis_findings has 5 rows for BG026. Simulated adding BG999 as a freshly-created same-name term; resolver returned matching_term_ids=[BG026, BG027, BG999]; findings.isin() pulled the 5 BG026 rows under the fresh term. PASS. Write path unchanged at L862 — new findings still get the current term_id as FK.
conclusion: Preserve historical FKs on writes; resolve by stable natural key on reads. term_name is the stable natural key for a business term across archive/re-create cycles. This pattern avoids schema churn (no foreign-key column swap, no migration) and also preserves audit trail (old BG026 rows stay tagged as BG026 so historical analytics remain correct). Resolution is O(number_of_glossary_rows_with_same_term_name), negligible in practice.

hypothesis: Fix A (hotfix 6) filtered selectors via _active_glossary but the POST-SELECTION lookup at three Business Glossary tabs still used the full glossary with iloc[0]. When an archived row and a draft row share the same display_name (archive-then-recreate-same-name flow), ORDER BY id puts the archived row first, and iloc[0] picks it — every tab resolves to the archived term even though the selector only offered the active one. User interpreted this as "Fix B analysis inheritance is working" because they saw archived-term data surface under what they thought was the new term. In reality they were just looking at BG026 directly; inheritance was never exercised.
what_we_did: Comprehensive app/ grep for post-selection glossary lookups. Classified each hit as SELECTOR (must use _active_glossary) / AUDIT (by explicit id — OK to leave) / LLM-PROMPT (should filter archived). Added _resolve_active_term(selected_name) helper in Business_Glossary.py that scopes to _active_glossary, defensively handles empty-match (error+stop) and multi-match (sort by created_date desc then iloc[0]). Replaced L2191 (Term Detail), L2345 (S2T Specification), L3123 (Data Quality) with helper calls. Separately patched three LLM-prompt context leaks in Data_Analysis.py (L571 Guided Analysis Term, L1105 Guided Analysis Domain, L1728 Domain Report) to filter archived rows before passing glossary_text to Claude. Total 6 fix sites. Audit lookups (metric_card tooltip L38, Data_Analysis L1604 L1776) intentionally unchanged.
what_happened: V1: "Active Deployed CPE Count" appears ONCE in _active_glossary (BG027) vs TWICE in full glossary (BG026 + BG027). V2/V3/V4: _resolve_active_term returns BG027 draft — Term Detail, S2T Specification, Data Quality tabs all resolve to the active term now. V5 FIX B EMPIRICALLY CONFIRMED: analysis_findings has 5 rows (AF039-AF043) keyed to archived BG026; inheritance resolver via term_name pulls them under fresh BG027 selection. Decision #65 is now not just correct-by-construction but empirically verified. V6 domain list unchanged (BG026/BG027 both domain=inventory). V7 audit lookup by explicit id still returns both terms intact for metric_card tooltip.
conclusion: Selector-lookup scope must match selector-list scope. Any post-selection resolution that feeds UI content must scope to the same filter as the list of options — mismatched scope lets non-listed rows win the lookup. Centralise the resolution in a helper (_resolve_active_term) so future selectors get correct behavior by default. LLM-prompt context is a separate scope question: archive usually wants exclusion (same filter as selector) unless the feature is explicitly about audit. RULE 42 revision emphasises project-wide applicability — audit every selector-lookup pair on any significant refactor.

hypothesis: Fix B (analysis_findings inheritance via term_name resolution) was implemented in an earlier hotfix (Data_Analysis.py L453-462) and extended to Business_Glossary.py L1095 under a follow-up hotfix. Intent was that a re-created term (post-archive) would inherit the archived sibling's profiling/validation findings so the user would not have to redo that LLM work. In practice the inheritance created multiple UI inconsistency bugs: Data Analysis tab showed the inherited findings but the S2T Specification tab guard did not, and even after propagation, the two tabs presented conflicting term_id contexts (archived vs active) depending on which screen surfaced first. User reviewed and decided: archive is final, clean semantic beats convenience.
what_we_did: Reverted both sites to naive business_term_id match. Data_Analysis.py L444-454 now does `term_findings = findings[findings['business_term_id'] == term_id]`. Business_Glossary.py `_load_term_analysis_findings` signature dropped the term_name parameter and the matching_ids resolver; body is a single load-all-then-filter by exact business_term_id. Stripped all "Fix B" / "inheritance" / "term_name resolution" comments. grep `matching_term_ids`, `isin(matching`, `Fix B` across app/ returns zero hits.
what_happened: BG027 (fresh draft, same term_name as archived BG026) now shows zero previous findings on Data Analysis tab — previously Fix B showed 5 inherited rows. S2T Specification tab still shows "Data analysis has not been performed" warning for BG027 — consistent with Data Analysis tab. BG026 (archived) retains its 5 findings as audit trail. Active terms with their own findings (e.g. BG007 7 findings) are unchanged. Active terms with no findings (e.g. BG023) unchanged.
conclusion: Archive is final — once a term is archived, its analysis_findings stay with the archived term_id as audit trail and do not follow a re-created same-named term. A re-created term starts with zero findings and must run fresh Guided Analysis. This produces a clean per-term-id semantic that matches how s2t_mapping and domain_facts already behave. See RULE 42 revision #5. Cross-term data bleeding was causing more bugs than it solved (decision #66 hotfix 8 caught the first; hotfix 8 extension caught the second; a third surfaced with analyst confusion about which term "owned" a profiling run).

hypothesis: The source-column classification work needs a per-column role metadata seed (source_column_roles) plus an invocation pipeline (scripts/classify_source_columns.py) that applies the 5-role classification rules to every raw_sap.* column, with RULE 21 cache-gating, RULE 37 write-boundary guard, RULE 38 LLM retry pattern, and user-override preservation across re-runs. The design specifies a five-stage pipeline, three invocation modes, and an audit-log-first write order.
what_we_did: Added two empty seeds (source_column_roles with 13 cols, source_column_role_changes with 11 cols, LF-only per RULE 34). Registered schema.yml tests (not_null, accepted_values for role/role_confidence/role_source/to_role/to_source, dbt_utils.unique_combination_of_columns on (table_name,column_name), expression_is_true for user_override_reason required when role_source='user_override'). Registered source_column_roles in app/_csv_safeguard.SAFEGUARDED_SEEDS with floor=10 (loosened from the designed steady-state floor of 495 so partial/--table runs aren't blocked — bump once cold-start steady-state known). Added _check_source_column_roles in scripts/_sidecar.py so check_artifact_staleness() supports the new artifact. Wired both new seeds into scripts/end_of_task.py scanner_seeds. Implemented scripts/classify_source_columns.py (~500 LOC) with all 5 stages plus apply_user_override() helper for the future Streamlit UI. Mechanical-rule pass consults sap_data_dictionary.data_type first (catches SAP DATS/QUAN/CURR/DEC codes that DuckDB stores as VARCHAR/DOUBLE) before falling back to DuckDB type.
what_happened: dbt seed --full-refresh --select source_column_roles source_column_role_changes → CREATE 0 for both (empty headers loaded cleanly). dbt test on both seeds → 18/18 PASS.
